does: This paper introduces Spectral Souping, a framework that enables Large Language Models to adapt to diverse individual user preferences without expensive online retraining. The core contribution is discovering a universal spectral representation within LLMs that facilitates efficient model merging. The method operates in two phases: first, it learns a set of specialized policy bases offline, each targeting distinct preference dimensions through standard training. Second, at inference time, an online adaptation algorithm rapidly merges these policies either through output combination or parameter interpolation to match specific user preferences. The approach avoids costly per-user retraining by leveraging the theoretical properties of the learned spectral representation, and experiments demonstrate substantial improvements over existing state-of-the-art alignment methods on preference alignment benchmarks.
connects: This work builds on the foundation of Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), which has become the standard approach for aligning LLMs with human preferences but treats users as a monolithic aggregate. The paper addresses limitations in existing personalized or multi-objective alignment methods that require expensive online adaptation or separate models per preference. It connects to recent research on model merging and ensemble methods that combine multiple specialized models, extending these ideas to the preference alignment domain. The spectral analysis perspective draws on interpretability work examining internal model representations and relates to compositional approaches in neural networks, combining these threads to enable efficient, dynamic preference adaptation without retraining.
enables: This framework opens several important research directions for personalized AI systems. It enables real-time adaptation to individual user preferences in deployed LLMs, making preference alignment more practical and scalable than methods requiring online optimization. The discovery of universal spectral structure could inspire further theoretical work on model compositionality and the geometry of preference space in neural networks. Potential follow-up work includes extending the method to handle conflicting or dynamically changing preferences, incorporating user feedback to refine the basis policies, and investigating whether similar spectral properties exist in other modalities or model architectures. The approach could also facilitate easier addition of new preference dimensions without retraining from scratch, moving toward more flexible and user-centric AI systems.
